item.title: Bridging Cardiovascular Physiology and Cognitive Science: Insights from Dr. Chueh-Lung ‘Laura’ Hwang
Mastering Social Dynamics: Reflections on Dr. Johanson’s (Coach J.) Communication Workshop
Fitness Instructor Teaching Methodology: Bridging Science and Practice
item.description:
As an Adjunct Lecturer in the Department of Leisure and Recreation Management at Taipei City University of Science and Technology, I focus on transforming a passion for fitness into a scientifically grounded career. My teaching methodology bridges the gap between exercise science and real-world application.

Foundational Science, Applied Technique, Program Design!

Fitness Instructor Teaching Methodology

Bridging the Gap Between Science and Application

In my course, Fitness Instructor Teaching Methodology, I guide students through the “Gold Standard” of the industry—the NSCA’s Essentials of Personal Training. My approach ensures that future professionals don’t just memorize terms but understand the “why” behind the “how.”

This instructional video provides an overview of how the course is structured to prepare students for the NSCA-CPT certification, focusing on anatomical adaptations, biomechanics, and the legal responsibilities of a fitness professional.

Watch Course Description on YouTube

Core Pillars of My Instruction

Based on my teaching demonstration, the curriculum is built around three critical domains:

  1. Foundational Science: We explore anatomy, bioenergetics, and biomechanics. It’s not just about naming muscles; it’s about understanding how the human body generates power and adapts to stress.
  2. Applied Technique: From stability exercises to advanced resistance training and cardiovascular protocols, students utilize hundreds of techniques outlined in the textbook and online resources to refine their coaching skills.
  3. Program Design: This is where science becomes art. Students learn to design safe, effective programs for diverse goals—from hypertrophy to aerobic endurance—and for Special Populations, including those with metabolic concerns, cardiovascular conditions, or spinal cord injuries.

Professional Excellence & Certification

The ultimate goal of this methodology is to ensure students are ready to pass professional examinations and lead clients toward their goals with absolute confidence.

  • Instructional Focus: Meticulously aligned with NSCA-CPT exam domains.
  • Practical Skills: Emphasizing business ethics and legal responsibilities to ensure career success.
  • Industry Expertise: Grounded in my experience as a PhD candidate and holder of NSCA-CSCS*D and ACSM-CEP certifications.

Did you find this page helpful? Consider sharing it 🙌

item.title: Weight Training Instruction Methodology: Bridging Science and Practice
item.description:
As an Adjunct Lecturer in the Department of Leisure and Recreation Management at Taipei City University of Science and Technology, I focus on transforming a passion for strength training into a scientifically grounded career. My teaching methodology bridges the gap between exercise science and practical weight training application.

Foundational Science, Applied Technique, Program Design!

Weight Training Instruction Methodology

Bridging the Gap Between Science and Application

In my course, Weight Training Instruction Methodology, I guide students through the essential principles of resistance training as outlined in the NSCA’s Essentials of Personal Training. My approach ensures that future professionals understand not only “how” to train clients but also “why” these methods are effective.

This instructional video provides an overview of how the course is structured to prepare students for NSCA-CPT certification, emphasizing muscular adaptations, biomechanics of lifts, and the safety and legal responsibilities of a fitness professional.

Watch Course Description on YouTube

Core Pillars of My Instruction

Based on my teaching demonstration, the curriculum is built around three critical domains specifically for weight training:

  1. Foundational Science: Students explore anatomy, muscle physiology, and biomechanics. Beyond identifying muscles, they learn how force is generated, how muscles adapt to resistance, and the science behind progressive overload.

  2. Applied Technique: From fundamental lifts like squats, deadlifts, and presses to advanced accessory exercises, students practice proper form, spotting, and coaching techniques to ensure safety and effectiveness.

  3. Program Design: This is where science becomes art. Students learn to create individualized strength programs for diverse goals—from hypertrophy to maximal strength—and for Special Populations, including those with orthopedic limitations, metabolic concerns, or post-injury rehabilitation needs.

Professional Excellence & Certification

The ultimate goal of this methodology is to ensure students are fully prepared to pass professional certification exams and lead clients toward their strength goals safely and effectively.

  • Instructional Focus: Aligned with NSCA-CPT exam domains, emphasizing resistance training principles.
  • Practical Skills: Covering coaching cues, injury prevention, and legal responsibilities for professional practice.
  • Industry Expertise: Grounded in my experience as a PhD candidate and holder of NSCA-CSCS*D and ACSM-CEP certifications.

Did you find this page helpful? Consider sharing it 🙌

item.title: A New Chapter in exercise science higher education career path
item.description:
【運科竹學術生涯新篇章：感謝與期待】

很榮幸在這個春季，獲聘擔任台北城市科技大學休閒事業系的兼任講師。

這段時間以來，從運動科學的學術研究到第一線的實務教學，我始終致力於將複雜的科學知識轉化為具備實踐價值的應用技術。這次將負責授課《健身教練指導》與《重量訓練指導法》，這兩門課結合了我過往的專業認證與訓練心得，希望能將這些知識傳遞給未來的產業生力軍。

感謝過去所有師長的提攜與這一路走來支持「我是運科竹」的你們。這不僅是一個講師職位的開始，更是我實踐「運動科學普及化」理想的另一個起點。期待在教室裡，與同學們一同探索運動訓練的奧秘！

從碩士班到現在的博士候選人，我的學習之路一直圍繞著「如何讓身體動得更有效率? 以及如何透過身體活動來增進認知功能？」這一核心。過去在研究室與實驗室的數據中打滾，與在健身房中面對不同訓練者的需求，這兩者的結合構成了我的教學理念。

這次受聘為講師，讓我重新檢視了自己的知識體系。如何用簡潔明瞭的方式引導學生？如何將專業的「肌力與體能」轉化為「能被執行的教練技術」？這是我在接下來課程中給自己的課題。教書，本質上也是另一種形式的科學傳播，希望能與學生們教學相長，在休閒事業系開創不一樣的教學火花。

#台北城市科技大學 #休閒事業系 #運動科學 #健身教練 #重量訓練 #我是運科竹

[A New Chapter in Sports Science Education]

I am honored to announce my appointment as an Adjunct Lecturer in the Department of Leisure Management at Taipei City University of Science and Technology this semester.

Bridging the gap between empirical sports science and real-world application has always been the cornerstone of my professional journey. Teaching “Fitness Instructor Advising” and “Guide for Weight Training” is a fantastic opportunity to translate my research background and certification expertise into actionable skills for the next generation of industry professionals.

I am deeply grateful for the mentorship I’ve received and the ongoing support from the community following “Coach Steve’s Performance.” This position represents more than just an academic appointment; it is a significant milestone in my commitment to making sports science accessible and practical. I look forward to an enriching semester of inquiry and growth with my students.

My academic trajectory has been fundamentally defined by one central question: How can we optimize human movement? & How can we improve our cognitive function through physical activity? Navigating through both the rigorous environment of research laboratories and the dynamic space of the gym floor has profoundly shaped my pedagogical approach.

Stepping into this role as a lecturer has provided a valuable opportunity to refine my expertise. My primary objective is to distill complex concepts—ranging from kinesiology to biomechanics—into practical, instruction-oriented techniques that students can confidently apply in their future careers. To me, teaching is an extension of scientific communication. I am eager to foster an interactive learning environment where, together with my students, we can elevate the standard of sports and fitness education.

#SportsScience #FitnessEducation #WeightTraining #AcademicJourney #LeisureManagement

item.title: Journal Meeting: Effect of sprint interval cycling with blood flow restriction on capillary blood lactate, cognitive function and perceptual response
item.description:
Presentation Highlights / 報告精華

Introduction & Rationale

While HIIT is effective for brain health, its high strain often leads to poor adherence. Blood Flow Restriction (BFR) combined with exercise has been proposed as a time-efficient alternative. This study explored if BFR coupled with “all-out” 10-second sprints (SIE) could enhance metabolic stress (lactate production) and subsequently improve cognitive functions like executive control.

Key Findings

  • Lactate Levels: Both BFR-SIE and SIE groups showed significant lactate increases (approx. 12 mmol/L), but BFR did not provide an additional boost over SIE alone.
  • Cognitive Performance: Reaction times in the Stroop Test improved in both conditions, but no significant changes were found in working memory (Digit Span).
  • Perceptual Strain: Participants in the BFR-SIE group reported significantly higher RPE (5.3 vs 4.9). The mechanical pressure likely stimulated Group III/IV afferents, reducing central motor drive and power output.

Practical Application

Applying BFR during maximal effort exercises may be suboptimal because the added discomfort can limit performance and motivation, offsetting the intended physiological benefits.

研究動機與背景

雖然高強度間歇運動（HIIT）對大腦健康有效，但其高強度常導致民眾難以堅持。結合血流限制（BFR）的訓練被視為一種高效的替代方案。本研究探討將 BFR 應用於「全速」10 秒衝刺（SIE），是否能透過增加代謝壓力（乳酸堆積）來進一步提升如執行控制等認知功能。

核心發現

  • 乳酸反應：BFR-SIE 與單純 SIE 組的乳酸皆顯著上升（約 12 mmol/L），但加入 BFR 並未如預期般產生更高的乳酸值。
  • 認知表現：兩組在 Stroop 測驗中的反應時間均有改善，但工作記憶（數字跨度測驗）則無顯著變化。
  • 自覺努力感：BFR-SIE 組的自覺耗力程度顯著較高（5.3 vs 4.9）。這顯示壓力帶來的身體不適感可能抑制了中樞運動驅動，導致功率輸出下降。

實務建議

在進行「最大努力」或「自發性強度」的運動時，加入 BFR 可能並非最佳選擇，因為額外的知覺壓力會限制運動表現與動機，進而抵消預期的生理獲益。

報告資訊

  • Presenter / 報告人: Ying-Chu Chen (陳映竹), Ph.D. Candidate
  • Advisor / 指導教授: Yu-Kai Chang (張育愷), Ph.D.
  • Lab / 實驗室: Physical Activity & Cognitive Neuroscience Lab (PACN Lab), NTNU
  • 影片連結: https://youtu.be/ye9k6n0T6yU

item.title: Advancing Global Pedagogy: Fulbright Taiwan EMI Workshop at Arizona State University
item.description:
During the Fulbright Taiwan EMI Overseas Professional Training Workshop at Arizona State University, Steve Ying-Chu Chen immersed himself in modern pedagogical strategies designed for the globalized classroom. From January 25 to February 7, 2026, the workshop focused on how English as a Medium of Instruction (EMI) can be leveraged to enhance learning outcomes without sacrificing content depth—specifically within the specialized fields of exercise science and cognitive neuroscience.

Key Workshop Focus Areas

  • Instructional Agility: Utilizing flexible bilingual strategies to help students engage with complex topics and jargon glossary, like inhibitory control and event-related potentials (ERP) in exercise science and health science.
  • Translanguaging in Practice: Moving beyond simple code-switching to allow students to draw on their full linguistic repertoire, deepening their understanding of scientific theory.
  • Educational Equity: Lowering language barriers to ensure that students from diverse backgrounds can participate equally in high-level academic discourse.

“Bilingual teaching is not just about switching between languages; it’s a powerful tool for promoting educational equity and global competence.”

Workshop Highlights and Media

POV: Experience the ASU EMI Workshop atmosphere.

Cultural immersion at the Heard Museum during the workshop.

Completion of two weeks of intensive training in Phoenix—officially certified!

Bridging Theory and Exercise Science

Steve demonstrated how EMI principles apply directly to the study of the brain and physical activity. By creating a multilingual environment, instructors can foster critical thinking and a stronger connection to global research networks. The seminar at ASU explored the future of bilingual collaboration, offering practical insights into finding the balance between academic rigor and linguistic accessibility.

This professional development experience underscores a commitment to advancing language inclusion and academic innovation, ensuring that the next generation of scholars is prepared for the international stage of scientific inquiry.

item.title: 榮獲國家運動科學中心 115 年度「博士生研究獎學金」 (Recipient of the 2026 Taiwan Institute of Sports Science Doctoral Research Scholarship)
item.description:
🏆 獲獎紀實 (Award Highlights)

國家運動科學中心（Taiwan Institute of Sports Science, TISS）為培育運動科學、運動醫學及運動科技人才，特設置「博士生研究獎學金」，旨在鼓勵並支持具有高度研究潛力之優秀在學博士候選人。中心希望透過此獎項，支持學生安心、專心從事研究工作。115 年度全台僅提供 4 名 獎學金名額，陳映竹憑藉其嚴謹的研究設計與學術潛力，順利通過審查名列其中。

The Taiwan Institute of Sports Science (TISS) established the “Doctoral Research Scholarship” to cultivate top-tier talents in sports science, sports medicine, and sports technology. The scholarship aims to encourage and support outstanding doctoral candidates with significant research potential, allowing them to focus entirely on their academic endeavors. For the 2026 academic year, only four scholarship slots were available nationwide, and Ying-Chu Chen was selected following a rigorous review process.

🔬 獲獎研究主題 (Award-Winning Research Topic)

「急性中等強度連續運動搭配血流限制對執行功能之影響：事件相關電位研究」 (The Effects of Acute Moderate-Intensity Continuous Exercise Combined with Blood Flow Restriction on Executive Function: An Event-Related Potential Study)

本研究聚焦於運動科學的前沿領域，探討 血流限制 (Blood Flow Restriction, BFR) 結合中等強度連續運動，如何影響個體的執行功能，並透過 事件相關電位 (ERP) 技術，從神經生理學的角度觀察大腦活動的即時變化。

This research focuses on the cutting-edge field of sports science, exploring how Blood Flow Restriction (BFR) combined with moderate-intensity continuous exercise affects executive functions. Using Event-Related Potential (ERP) technology, the study observes real-time changes in brain activity from a neurophysiological perspective.

🎓 意義與願景 (Significance and Vision)

獲此殊榮不僅是中心對映竹過去研究成果的肯定，更是對其未來在運動科學領域貢獻的深切期許。這份獎學金將支持映竹在博士候選人階段，持續推動具備學術價值與實務應用潛力的研究工作。

Receiving this prestigious scholarship is not only a validation of Ying-Chu’s previous research achievements by TISS but also an expectation for his future contributions to the field of sports science. This support will allow him, as a doctoral candidate, to continue advancing research with high academic value and practical application potential.

(資料來源：改寫自國家運動科學中心 115 年度獲獎名單公告與學術研究計畫 / 2026-01-16)

item.title: 榮耀時刻：入圍第六屆好好聽FM播客大賽！【運科竹愛科普｜運動讓腦袋變年輕？】 (Glory Moment: Finalist of the 6th Hao Hao Ting FM Podcast Competition!)
item.description:
🎙️ 評分機制分析 (Scoring Mechanism Analysis)

映竹能在眾多作品中脫穎而出，關鍵在於對評分指標的精確掌握： Ying-Chu’s success in the competition stems from his precise mastery of the following criteria:

  1. 作品主題及創意 (Theme & Creativity - 50%)： 選題「運動讓腦袋變年輕」直擊現代人健康需求，並以「肌肉激素」作為科普亮點。 (The topic “Exercise Makes the Brain Younger” addresses modern health needs, utilizing “Myokines” as a scientific highlight.)
  2. 主持及表述技巧 (Hosting & Presentation - 30%)： 展現了運動科學研究者的自信與親和力。 (Demonstrated the confidence and approachability of an exercise science researcher.)
  3. 聲音表情及音質音色 (Vocal Expression & Audio Quality - 15%)： 具備層次感的聲線提供了極佳的聆聽體驗。 (A layered vocal tone provided an excellent listening experience.)
  4. 音樂音效或多元語言 (Music, Effects & Language - 5%)： 襯托出節目的節奏感與完成度。 (Enhanced the rhythm and overall production quality of the program.)

🌍 學術轉譯的意義 (The Significance of Science Communication)

在這次的入圍節目中，我特別聚焦於 肌肉激素 (Myokines)。這些由肌肉纖維分泌的蛋白質，是身體與大腦溝通的橋樑。當我們運動時，這些激素會釋放進入血液，幫助我們維護神經健康與認知功能。

In this nominated episode, I specifically focused on Myokines. These proteins secreted by muscle fibers serve as a bridge between the body and the brain. During exercise, these hormones are released into the bloodstream, helping to maintain neurological health and cognitive function.

📜 競賽資訊摘要 (Competition Overview)

  • 大賽主題 (Theme)：【好膽你來講 (kóng) / Dare to Speak】。
  • 目前成績 (Current Status)：榮獲入圍，並持續在人氣排行中衝刺。 (Finalist; currently competing in the popularity rankings.)
  • 核心精神 (Core Spirit)：追求「好內容」以「好的表述」傳遞給大眾。 (Seeking “Good Content” delivered through “Excellent Expression.”)

(資料來源：改寫自「好好聽FM」大賽評分說明與「我是運科竹」活動紀實 / 2026-02-13)

item.title: Attending Professor Shih-Chun Kao’s lecture on “Brain and cognition in response to cognitively engaging exercise”
榮獲114學年度呂聯宗呂王敏華夫婦獎助學基金 (Recipient of the 114th Academic Year Lu Lien-Tsung & Lu Wang Min-Hua Scholarship)
item.description:
🏆 English Version

Congratulations to Ying-Chu Chen for receiving the 114th Academic Year Lu Lien-Tsung & Lu Wang Min-Hua Scholarship!

The award ceremony was held on December 26, 2025, at the Department of Physical Education and Sport Sciences, National Taiwan Normal University (NTNU). The scholarship was personally presented by the distinguished alumnus Mr. Lu Lien-Tsung (Class of ‘52) and his wife, Mrs. Lu Wang Min-Hua, who traveled from Tainan to honor the recipients.

Event Highlights:

  • A Legacy of Gratitude: Mr. and Mrs. Lu have established a perpetual fund exceeding one million USD to support the department. Their core philosophy is “Sustainability” and “Gratitude” (Drinking water while thinking of its source).
  • Words of Wisdom: During the ceremony, Mr. Lu encouraged students to stay grounded while keeping a broad vision. He emphasized that in the era of AI and rapid change, “Interdisciplinary learning is no longer an elective, but a requirement.”
  • Future Vision: He urged students to build both “hard” and “soft” skills, emphasizing that proficiency in English and professional subjects are merely the basics. True success comes from the wisdom to make the right choices and the ability to learn across fields.

Department Chair, Professor Chang Yu-Kai, highlighted that the Lu family are not just providers of resources but role models of integrity and foresight. This scholarship aims to pass on these values to the next generation.

🇹🇼 中文報導

恭喜陳映竹榮獲114學年度呂聯宗呂王敏華夫婦獎助學基金！

國立臺灣師範大學體育與運動科學系於2025年12月26日舉辦頒獎典禮。52級校友呂聯宗學長與呂王敏華賢伉儷特地自臺南返回母系，親自頒發獎助學金並勉勵學弟妹，展現對母校深厚的情感與長遠的承諾支持。

典禮紀實與師長勉勵：

  • 永續支持與價值傳承： 系主任張育愷講座教授指出，呂學長夫婦設立之獎助學基金已逾百萬美元規模，並以「永續支持」為核心理念。這份獎學金的意義不僅止於經費支持，更在於將凡事預先規劃、對承諾負責的態度，傳遞給每一位年輕世代。

  • 跨域學習與智慧選擇： 呂聯宗學長特別提點學弟妹，在AI當道與局勢快速變動的今天，精進英文與專業學科僅是基本功。他語重心長地強調：「跨域已不再是選修課（應然），而是必修課（必然）。」 唯有具備跨域學習的能力與做出正確選擇的智慧，才能安身立命。

  • 飲水思源： 呂學長勉勵獲獎學生，期盼同學在接受幫助的同時，能記得來時的起點，並在未來有能力時，將這份善意持續傳承。

(資料來源：改寫自國立臺灣師範大學公共事務中心新聞稿 / 2025-12-31)

item.title: Attending Professor Hideaki Soya’s lecture on “Hormetic Effects of Exercise on Cognition: A Translational Research”

item.description:
Congratulations to Ying-Chu Chen for receiving the 2025 TOEFL ITP Higher Education Academic Research Scholarship! This distinction serves as a testament to her dedication and outstanding results in the field of academic research.

item.title: 2025第九屆師大運動文青獎 (2025 9th NTNU Sports Literature and Photography Award)
item.description:
Congratulations to Ying-Chu Chen for winning 2nd Place in the English Prose Category at the 2025 9th NTNU Sports Literature and Photography Award (2025第九屆師大運動文青獎)!

Her winning submission, titled “Breaking Limits: The Profound Impact of Weight Training on Personal Growth,” explores the transformative power of strength training, transcending physical boundaries to foster personal development and resilience.

Ceremony Details

  • Date: December 18, 2025 (Thursday)
  • Time: 12:20 - 14:00
  • Location: Gold Medal Lecture Hall, 3rd Floor, Gymnasium, National Taiwan Normal University
    （國立臺灣師範大學體育館三樓金牌講堂）

The ceremony celebrated the best literary and artistic works related to sports culture at NTNU. We extend our gratitude to the NTNU Sports Literature and Photography Award Organizing Team for this honor.

item.title: 國立臺灣師範大學圖書館114學年度第一學期圖書館自主英語學習讀書會
item.description:
Ying-Chu Chen actively participated in the Library Autonomous English Learning Study Group hosted by the National Taiwan Normal University Library during the first semester of the 114th academic year. The theme for this semester was “Cybernetics”, a highly interdisciplinary field exploring control and communication in animals and machines. The sessions were led by Ph.D. Candidate Richard McLawhorn, whose expertise provided a robust academic framework for the discussions.

The program offered participants a rigorous intellectual environment to improve their English skills while grappling with complex theoretical concepts. Throughout the sessions, Ying-Chu demonstrated exceptional initiative and intellectual curiosity. Given the abstract nature of Cybernetics, she meticulously prepared for each meeting by analyzing the assigned readings on feedback loops, homeostasis, and system dynamics. This preparation allowed her to actively engage in high-level discussions and share her insights on how these mechanisms apply to various fields.

Under Richard McLawhorn’s guidance, the study group moved beyond simple conversation to deep analytical exchanges. The sessions incorporated critical analysis of texts and systemic modeling discussions. For instance, during the exploration of feedback mechanisms, Ying-Chu actively exchanged ideas with other members, debating the implications of positive and negative feedback in biological and social systems.

The all-English discussion environment presented a significant challenge, particularly regarding the specific terminology of Cybernetics. Initially, articulating complex systemic relationships in English was demanding. However, through consistent practice and Richard’s encouragement, Ying-Chu’s confidence and ability to express abstract ideas improved significantly. Engaging with participants from diverse academic backgrounds further broadened her horizons, allowing her to see Cybernetics as a bridge between the humanities and sciences.

For Ying-Chu, this study group was a transformative experience. She concluded that delving into Cybernetics not only refined her academic English proficiency but also equipped her with a systems-thinking mindset, providing a fresh perspective to approach future research and professional challenges.

item.title: The 16th ISSP World Congress: Acute Resistance Exercise and Inhibitory Control
item.description:
Certificate of Attendance: Mr. Ying-Chu Chen participated in The 16th ISSP World Congress, sponsored by the Shine Tak Foundation and hosted by the Hong Kong Sports Institute between 8 and 12 December 2025.

Presentation Highlights: In his oral presentation, “The Influence of Acute Resistance Exercise Volume on Inhibitory Control: A Randomized Controlled Trial,” Ying-Chu Chen discussed the relationship between resistance training variables and executive function. The study utilized a randomized controlled design to compare the effects of different exercise intensities with matched total volumes.

Key Findings:

  • Volume Matters: The study concluded that exercise volume might be a key determinant for acute cognitive benefits.
  • Intensity Agnostic: Both low and moderate-intensity resistance exercise protocols resulted in significant improvements in inhibitory control (measured by the Stroop Task) when the total volume was held constant.
  • Physiological Response: Lactate levels increased similarly across exercise groups, suggesting a comparable metabolic response supporting the cognitive gains.
  • Clinical Implication: Low-intensity resistance exercise can be an effective strategy for enhancing cognitive health, especially for populations unable to perform high-intensity training, provided the volume is sufficient.

This research contributes to the growing body of evidence in exercise cognition interaction, offering practical guidelines for exercise prescription aimed at brain health.

item.title: EMI Teaching Course Demonstration
item.description:
Steve Ying-Chu Chen is designed to facilitate a supportive EMI learning environment. You will receive insights into how I structure academic literacy within the field of Sport Sciences.

Clear Objectives, Scaffolded Learning, Active Participation!

On this page, you’ll find the EMI course demonstration of how I utilize digital resources to teach core concepts like Experimental Research Design and the distinction between Acute exercise and Cognitive Function.

Physical Activity and Cognitive Function

Visual Scaffolding for Complex Concepts

In my EMI course demonstration, Physical Activity and Cognitive Function, I utilize visual aids to explain abstract methodological concepts. [cite_start]As demonstrated in my teaching presentation, visualizing the relationship between the Independent Variable (Cause) and the Dependent Variable (Effect) is crucial for student understanding.

This video serves as a flipped-classroom resource to help students visualize how we manipulate variables—such as comparing High-Intensity Interval Training (HIIT) vs. Moderate Continuous Training—to measure outcomes like short-term memory.

Watch on YouTube

Youtube:

{{< youtube X1pHnvnacZA >}}

Clear Objectives, Scaffolded Learning, Active Participation!

On this page, you’ll find examples of how I utilize digital resources to teach core concepts like Statistical Analysis and Experimental Design.

Linear Regression Course Guided Reading EMI course Demonstration

Simplifying Abstract Concepts with Analogies

Teaching statistics in an EMI setting can be challenging due to the complex terminology. In this “Guided Reading” session, I demonstrate how to use a relatable Real-world Analogy—the “Lunch Scenario”—to explain the concept of Ordinary Least Squares (OLS) and its sensitivity to outliers.

By asking students to imagine 9 people buying a $10 lunch versus 1 person buying a $500 meal, I help them visualize how a single data point can distort the regression line. This Analogy-based Scaffolding is a key strategy I use to make abstract mathematical models accessible to students.

Youtube:

{{< youtube OFHVSfIVx-0 >}}

Did you find this page helpful? Consider sharing it 🙌

item.title: Attending Professor Yongchul Chung’s lecture on “Lessons Learned from 20+ Year Consulting Journey”
Cybernetics English Reading Clubs
item.description:
Ying-Chu Chen recently participated in an English Reading Club session that embarked on an intellectual exploration of cybernetics, a profoundly influential, if often misunderstood, interdisciplinary field. The group began by defining the discipline as coined by Norbert Wiener: the study of regulatory mechanisms, communication, and control within complex systems, whether living organisms, machines, or social structures.

The reading group’s discussion transcended the pop-culture caricature of cyborgs. Instead, participants, including Ying-Chu, engaged with the foundational principles that govern how systems self-regulate, adapt, and pursue goals. Their textual analysis centered on the ubiquitous concept of the feedback loop, the mechanism by which a system’s output is “fed back” as input. They dissected seminal works to distinguish between deviation-correcting (negative) feedback, which ensures stability (homeostasis), and deviation-amplifying (positive) feedback, which drives exponential change.

Furthermore, the attendees grappled with the field’s sophisticated evolution, contrasting “first-order” cybernetics (the objective observation of external systems) with “second-order” cybernetics, which compellingly implicates the observer within the system being studied. The group acknowledged that this framework is no mere historical artifact but rather the conceptual progenitor of modern artificial intelligence, systems biology, computational neuroscience, and management theory.

The discussions challenged the participants to synthesize these abstract models and apply them rigorously, collectively exploring how these concepts illuminate cognitive processes, ecological resilience, or the emergent behavior of algorithms. It was a high-level discourse that successfully dissected the complex architecture of control underpinning the modern world.

item.title: Journal Meeting: Effectiveness of short-term cycling interventions in older adults
Shaping the Future of Bilingual Collaboration: EMI Course Demonstration by Steve Ying-Chu Chen
Shaping the Future of Bilingual Collaboration: EMI Course Demonstration by Steve Ying-Chu Chen
item.description:
In the course demonstration session led by Steve Ying-Chu Chen, he showcased his extensive teaching experience and demonstrated how flexible bilingual strategies in EMI (English as a Medium of Instruction) can help students engage more effectively in fields like exercise science and cognitive neuroscience. By using translanguaging, students are able to draw on their full linguistic resources to deepen their understanding and express complex ideas, while also enhancing their academic English skills.

Ying-Chu Chen emphasized that bilingual teaching is not just about switching between languages; it’s a powerful tool for promoting educational equity and global competence. When students are taught in a multilingual environment, they can develop a broader perspective, critical thinking skills, and a stronger connection to global knowledge.

The seminar also addressed the growing importance of language equity in higher education, stressing that EMI should aim to lower language barriers so that students from diverse linguistic backgrounds can equally participate, understand, and express themselves. By balancing language use with content delivery, instructors can create a more inclusive learning environment that is both academically rigorous and accessible.

Additionally, the seminar explored the future of bilingual collaboration in teaching, offering practical strategies and insights to help educators find the balance between theory and practice in EMI classrooms. Overall, the event not only showcased the diversity of bilingual education but also prompted attendees to think about how to continue advancing language inclusion and academic innovation in globalized education.

item.title: Toastmasters 示範例會心得：從結構、回饋到自信的全新理解
The 4th BESTEP Elite Award (Third Prize, Silver Certificate) - Ying-Chu Chen
item.description:
中文公告

熱烈祝賀陳映竹榮獲第四屆「培力英檢」菁英獎（菁英三獎）

在教育部補助語言訓練測驗中心（LTTC）辦理的第四屆「培力英檢菁英獎」評選中，映竹表現優異，完成培力英檢聽、讀、說、寫四項測驗，四項成績全數達到 CEFR B2 等級【bestep.tw】。 根據培力英檢菁英獎辦法，菁英三獎由四項能力均達 CEFR B2 的考生獲頒，並附贈銀色證書【bestep.tw】。映竹的持續努力和卓越成果，正是培力英檢鼓勵學生均衡提升英語聽、說、讀、寫四項能力宗旨的最佳實踐。對他的成就深感驕傲，期勉他持續精進，也期待更多同學踴躍參加培力英檢，提升自身英語力。

English Announcement

Congratulations to Ying‑Chu Chen on Winning the 4th BESTEP Elite Award (Third Prize)

We are delighted to announce that Ying‑Chu Chen, has earned the Third‑Tier Elite Award in the 4th BESTEP Elite Awards. The awards, organized by Taiwan’s Ministry of Education and the Language Training and Testing Center (LTTC), require candidates to achieve CEFR B2 in listening, reading, speaking, and writing within a year【bestep.tw】. Ying‑Chu achieved B2 in all four skills and therefore received the Third Prize (菁英三獎) and a Silver Certificate【bestep.tw】. The BESTEP Elite Awards aim to encourage university students to develop balanced English proficiency, and Ying‑Chu’s dedication exemplifies this goal. We extend our heartfelt congratulations and hope his success inspires more students to enhance their language skills through BESTEP!

Enhancing Research Literacy and Global Perspective Through Collaborative Learning
Knowledge monetization
item.description:
Ying Chu Che’s CV website is designed to give knowledge-based workers a seamless experience. You will receive numerous potential application insight of knowledge.

Your Knowledge, Your Platform, Your Future!

On this page, you’ll find some examples of the types of real application that can be rendered within your field.

YouTube Video

Teach your course or sell your idea by sharing videos with your potential clients. Providing valuable content will build trust, establish your authority, and attract the right audience who are genuinely interested in what you offer. Whether you’re sharing insights, tutorials, or behind-the-scenes processes, consistent and engaging videos can turn your knowledge into a powerful tool for connection and conversion.

Youtube:

{{< youtube a-jPzFZW5_c >}}

Podcast

You’ll gain insights into personal training and exercise science through my podcast, where evidence-based knowledge meets real-world application.

In each episode, I share expert perspectives on fitness, health, and human performance—combining scientific research with practical tips for trainers, athletes, and health-conscious individuals. Whether you’re looking to deepen your understanding of biomechanics, training strategies, or the latest findings in exercise and sport sciences, this podcast is your gateway to informed, actionable knowledge. 我是運科竹 channel:

👉 Click to listen the channel!
我是運科竹 Podcast

Vocus and Medium writing

Vocus and Medium are two dynamic publishing platforms that empower individuals to share their knowledge, perspectives, and expertise with a global audience. Whether you’re a thought leader, educator, researcher, or entrepreneur, these platforms offer intuitive tools to transform your insights into engaging articles, essays, and narratives.

Vocus is widely adopted in Mandarin-speaking communities and is known for its reader-centric features, monetization opportunities, and strong social engagement functions. 我是運科竹 Vocus

Medium, on the other hand, offers a sleek, minimalist interface and access to a vast international network of readers, professionals, and niche communities. It’s an ideal space for English-language writing, especially if you’re aiming to build authority and connect globally. 我是運科竹 Medium

Both platforms prioritize authenticity, storytelling, and value-driven content, making them excellent choices for knowledge monetization and personal branding.

Did you find this page helpful? Consider sharing it 🙌

113學年度第二學期宗教研究獎學金 (2025_Spring Religion Research Scholarship)
item.description:
“Congratulations to Ying-Chu Chen for receiving the Religion Research Scholarship for the second semester of the 113th academic year from the Department of English at National Taiwan Normal University! The scholarship recognizes students with strong academic performance (GPA above 3.40 or average grade above 80) and excellence in religious-themed literary or research works in Chinese, including essays, poetry, fiction, or analytical pieces rooted in Buddhist, Christian, or Catholic traditions and aligned with the moral values of the Five Precepts, Ten Good Deeds, or Ten Commandments.

Ying-Chu’s award-winning submission was a book review published in an A&HCI-indexed journal: Book review: Feng Chuan PAN. Journey to the West of Filial Piety: Translation of Filial Piety and Origin of European Sinology. Taipei: Linking Publishing Company, 2022. 552 pp. This outstanding work reflects deep engagement with cross-cultural religious scholarship and demonstrates Ying-Chu’s academic strength in the intersection of literature, translation, and religious studies.”

item.description: Congratulations to Ying-Chu Chen for once again being awarded the prestigious Lü Lien-Tzung and Lü Wang Min-Hua Scholarship Fund for the 113th academic year! This recognition follows Ying-Chu’s outstanding achievement in obtaining two highly esteemed international certifications, the Clinical Exercise Physiologist Certification from the American College of Sports Medicine (ACSM) and the Physical Activity in Public Health Specialist (PAPHS) certification, jointly awarded by the ACSM and the National Society of Physical Activity Practitioners in Public Health (NSPAPPH).

item.title: 國立臺灣師範大學圖書館113學年度第一學期圖書館自主英語學習讀書會
item.description: Ying-Chu Chen actively participated in the Library Autonomous English Learning Study Group hosted by the National Taiwan Normal University Library during the first semester of the 113th academic year. The theme of the study group was “Career Plan”, focusing on the importance of career planning, the process of exploring personality traits, and strategies for identifying suitable career paths. This program offered participants a comprehensive and systematic approach to improving their English skills while gaining valuable insights into career development.Throughout the sessions, Ying-Chu demonstrated exceptional initiative and dedication. She meticulously prepared for each meeting by thoroughly reading the assigned English materials, taking detailed notes, and reflecting on related questions. This preparation allowed his to actively engage in discussions and share his insights during the sessions. Ying-Chu believed that this proactive approach not only enhanced his English reading and communication skills but also deepened his understanding of the core concepts of career planning, laying a solid foundation for his future career choices.The study group incorporated a variety of interactive activities, such as group discussions, role-playing, and personal sharing, to enrich the participants’ learning experience. For instance, during the exploration of personality traits, participants completed a series of personality assessments. Through these tests, Ying-Chu gained a clearer understanding of his strengths and challenges, enabling his to reflect on how these traits could align with his future career goals. Additionally, when analyzing suitable career types, she actively exchanged ideas with othis members, gaining diverse perspectives that helped his reassess his career choices and develop a deeper appreciation for the diversity of the professional world.The all-English discussion environment presented both challenges and opportunities for Ying-Chu. Initially, expressing his views in English was somewhat difficult, but with each session, his confidence and oral proficiency improved significantly. Engaging with participants from various academic disciplines furthis broadened his horizons by exposing his to diverse viewpoints and experiences. For Ying-Chu, this study group was more than just a language-learning experience; it was a valuable opportunity for self-discovery and career exploration. She concluded that such activities not only equip participants with practical tools for career planning but also instill greater courage and wisdom to face the uncertainties of the future.

item.title: 113學年度第一學期優秀研究生獎學金 (113_Fall Most Outstanding Student Award)
item.description: “Congratulations to Ying-Chu Chen! for receiving the Outstanding Graduate Student Scholarship for the first semester of the 113th academic year from the Department of Physical Education and Sport Sciences at National Taiwan Normal University! This semester, Ying-Chu has achieved remarkable academic accomplishments, including the publication of one first-authored article in a TSSCI-indexed journal in Chinese, one co-authored article in an SCI-indexed journal in English, and a book review in a Chinese A&HCI-indexed journal. Additionally, Ying-Chu has obtained the prestigious Clinical Exercise Physiologist Certification from the American College of Sports Medicine (ACSM) and earned certification as a Physical Activity in Public Health Specialist (PAPHS) through the ACSM and the National Society of Physical Activity Practitioners in Public Health (NSPAPPH).”

item.title: Career Path English Reading Clubs with Yolanda
item.description:
Steve Ying-Chu Chen recently participated in an English Reading Club session hosted by Yolanda, which embarked on a rigorous intellectual exploration of the modern career trajectory—a domain often reduced to platitudes yet profoundly significant in its complexity. The group began by redefining the concept of a “career” not as a linear ladder, but as a dynamic ecosystem of professional identity, adaptability, and value exchange within an increasingly volatile marketplace.

Under Yolanda’s astute moderation, the discussion transcended the pedestrian clichés of resume polishing or networking tactics. Instead, participants, including Steve, engaged with the foundational principles that govern professional self-regulation and goal attainment. Their analysis centered on the pivotal concept of “strategic agility,” dissecting the mechanisms by which individuals must process market feedback to iterate on their skill sets. They examined the dichotomy between “maintenance learning,” which ensures stability in a current role, and “shock learning,” which is required to navigate the exponential changes driven by technological disruption.

Furthermore, the attendees grappled with the sophisticated evolution of vocational psychology, contrasting the antiquated “organizational” career model (where the institution dictates the path) with the “protean” career model. This latter framework compellingly implicates the individual as the sole architect of their journey, driven by psychological success rather than external metrics. The group acknowledged that this paradigm shift is the conceptual progenitor of the gig economy, the portfolio career, and the rise of personal branding.

The discussions challenged the participants to synthesize these abstract models and apply them rigorously to their own lives, collectively exploring how these concepts illuminate the challenges of AI displacement, the necessity of lifelong upskilling, and the pursuit of purpose. It was a high-level discourse that successfully dissected the complex architecture of professional navigation underpinning the modern world.

Passed the ACSM CEP certification!
item.description: Congratulations are certainly in order! It’s with immense pride that we announce Ying-Chu Chen’s successful passage of the ACSM CEP certification. This accomplishment underscores Ying-Chu’s dedication, perseverance, and expertise in the field of exercise physiology and fitness training. The ACSM CEP certification is widely recognized as a benchmark of excellence in the industry, and Ying-Chu’s attainment of this prestigious credential reflects her commitment to maintaining the highest standards of professional competence. With this achievement, Ying-Chu not only demonstrates her mastery of essential concepts and principles but also her readiness to positively impact the lives of others through evidence-based exercise programming and personalized training regimens. As Ying-Chu continues to advance her career and broaden her impact, her success serves as an inspiration to colleagues and clients alike, motivating them to pursue their own goals with passion and determination. We applaud Ying-Chu for her exceptional achievement and look forward to witnessing the continued impact of her expertise in promoting health and wellness within our community.

item.title: Explorations in Poetic Transcendence with Ruben
item.description:
Steve Ying-Chu Chen recently participated in an English Reading Club session hosted by Ruben, which embarked on a rigorous intellectual exploration of poetic form and its capacity to encapsulate the human condition—a domain often dismissed as mere abstraction, yet profoundly significant in its linguistic and cognitive complexity. The group began by redefining “poetry” not as a static arrangement of verse, but as a dynamic interplay of phonology, semantics, and emotional resonance.

Under Ruben’s astute moderation, the discussion transcended the pedestrian clichés of rhyming schemes or basic imagery. Instead, participants, including Steve, engaged with the foundational principles that govern “poetic economy”—the mechanism by which maximum affect is achieved through minimal, yet precise, linguistic input. Their analysis centered on the concept of “metaphorical salience,” dissecting how poets utilize cross-domain mapping to bridge the gap between tangible experience and metaphysical inquiry. They examined the dichotomy between “formalist precision,” which demands structural rigor, and “negative capability,” the capacity to remain in uncertainties and mysteries without an irritable reaching after fact and reason.

Furthermore, the attendees grappled with the sophisticated evolution of contemporary poetics, contrasting the traditional “mimetic” model (where poetry reflects external reality) with the “expressive” and “objective” models. This latter framework compellingly positions the poem as an autonomous object of inquiry, where the individual’s perception is both the architect and the subject of the literary experience. The group acknowledged that this paradigm shift is the conceptual progenitor of modern experimental verse and the reclamation of oral traditions in the digital age.

The discussions challenged the participants to synthesize these abstract models and apply them rigorously to their own cognitive frameworks, collectively exploring how poetry illuminates the nuances of human empathy, the limitations of literal language, and the enduring pursuit of aesthetic truth. It was a high-level discourse that successfully dissected the complex architecture of verbal art underpinning our shared cultural consciousness.

item.title: Effect of acute concurrent exercise training and the mediating role of lactate on executive function: An ERP study
單次同步運動對於認知執行功能之影響
item.description:
認知執行功能在日常生活當中扮演相當重要的角色，認知執行功能是指一系列高階心理過程，包括注意力控制、工作記憶、認知靈活性、問題解決能力以及抑制控制等。這些功能協助我們處理複雜的資訊、適應環境變化以及有效地規劃和執行任務。在日常生活中，認知執行功能扮演著關鍵的角色，影響著我們的學習、工作、人際關係和整體生活品質。像是：

1. 工作和學習：在職場和學習環境中，我們需要處理大量資訊、優先安排任務、集中注意力，並持續保持高度的認知靈活性。良好的認知執行功能有助於提升工作效率和學習成果。工作記憶的強大意味著我們能夠暫時儲存並處理信息，這對於解決複雜的問題和執行多步驟任務至關重要。

2. 人際關係：與他人互動需要我們不僅理解對方的情感和意圖，還需要適當地表達自己的觀點。認知執行功能可以幫助我們保持專注，避免分心，並在溝通中選擇合適的言語和行為反應。同時，它還有助於我們理解和掌握社交規則，從而建立良好的人際關係。

3. 自我管理與情緒調控：良好的認知執行功能有助於我們抵制衝動，從而更好地管理情緒和行為。在面對壓力和挑戰時，我們可以利用這些功能來冷靜思考、制定解決方案，而不是受情緒支配。這種情緒調控有助於維護心理健康，減少情緒爆發和衝突。

4. 日常生活技能：從買菜做飯到交通導航，我們的日常生活充滿了需要認知執行功能的活動。注意力控制有助於我們專注於重要任務，不受干擾。抑制能力使我們能夠避免不必要的行為或說話。認知靈活性則允許我們適應不同的環境和情境。

5. 老年健康：隨著年齡的增長，認知執行功能的衰退可能會影響老年人的生活。保持這些功能的良好狀態有助於延緩認知衰退的速度，並提高老年人的生活質量。訓練和鍛煉這些功能可以幫助保持大腦的靈活性和活力。

總而言之，認知執行功能是我們在面對複雜任務、挑戰和日常生活中所需的關鍵心理能力。它們影響著我們的思考方式、情緒管理、人際關係以及生活品質。這篇研究發現: 單次同步運動可以提升成年男性訊息處理速度，但是在抑制控制則與阻力運動無顯著差異，對於其它族群之影響，目前則尚待更多研究釐清！

item.title: 陳映竹擔任惠瑜慈善協會課後輔導志工
item.description: 雖然沒有餘裕的財富，但有著相關人文學科專業知識和無比的服務熱情，希冀能夠為偏鄉孩童以及弱勢族群們提供更多可能的學習機會和不求回饋的無私支持。對我來說，擔任一對一課後輔導志工，所得到的比付出得到更多。藉由擔任課後輔導志工，看著孩子們的學業成績不斷進步、學習自信心也倍增，透過短暫的陪伴和無私的關心讓弱勢學生感受到了被重視和被認同的溫暖，我想這些對他們的生涯成長和未來人格發展都是至關重要的。雖然沒有餘裕的財富，但有著相關人文學科專業知識和無比的服務熱情，希冀能夠為偏鄉孩童以及弱勢族群們提供更多可能的學習機會和不求回饋的無私支持。對我來說，擔任一對一課後輔導志工，所得到的比付出得到更多。藉由擔任課後輔導志工，看著孩子們的學業成績不斷進步、學習自信心也倍增，透過短暫的陪伴和無私的關心讓弱勢學生感受到了被重視和被認同的溫暖，我想這些對他們的生涯成長和未來人格發展都是至關重要的。 也感謝國立政治大學中國文學系紮實的中國文學系統性訓練，讓我能夠在畢業後將近二十餘年的時間，依舊能夠從容的幫高中三年級的學生複習高中國文！國立政治大學中國文學系以完善的師資陣容，無論是在傳統小學、現代詩詞小學，或者是義理之學，皆有著完善且獨特的知識體系與研究內容，也為其學生打造了健全的未來發展體系！

item.title: Attend International Academic Conferences in ACSM 70th Annual Meeting
item.description:
恭喜陳映竹榮獲國科會以及國立臺灣師範大學研究發展處出席國際會議補助，前往美國丹佛，出席美國運動醫學會第七十屆年會，並進行海報發表，內容為「Acute Effect of Combined Exercise on Inhibit Control: A Preliminary Study 」，並且於會議過程當中，展現絕佳自信與英語溝通能力，讓健身運動與認知功能研究能夠於世界展露頭角！本次陳映竹海報發表總共兩篇：

  1. Chen, Y.-C., Leung, T.-S., Chang, Y.-C., Yung, Y.-W., Chang, Y.-K., Chen, C.-Y. (2023, May 30–June 2). Acute Effect Of Combined Exercise On Inhibit Control: A Preliminary Study [Poster Presentation]. The 70th Annual Meeting of American College of Sport Medicine (ACSM), Denver City, Colorado, USA, 2023. https://www.acsm.org/annual-meeting/annual-home

  2. Chen, M.-T., Liao, Y.-H., Chen, Y.-C., Leung, T.-S., Chang, Y.-C., Yung, Y.-W., Chen, C.-Y. (2023, May 30–June 2). Effects of Acute Concurrent Exercise on Cognitive Performance and Related Regulation Hormones [Poster Presentation]. The 70th Annual Meeting of American College of Sport Medicine (ACSM), Denver City, Colorado, USA, 2023. https://www.acsm.org/annual-meeting/annual-home

item.title: How to exercise in the COVID-19 pandemic?
item.description: 疫情期間，許多人由於政府政策以及居家隔離等因素，進而必須暫停或者中斷健身運動，或者是想藉由身體活動以及健身運動來增加自身的免疫力。那麼，該如何透過簡單居家運動來達到這樣的效果呢？國立臺北護理健康大學運動保健學系廖翊宏教授兼任系主任認為規律進行有氧耐力運動訓練者，其呼吸肌功能將會相較於沒有進行規律訓練者要來得較好！並且人們可以透過簡單的居家訓練動作，無論是肌力訓練或者是有氧耐力訓練，來達到健身運動訓練效果。此外，也可以透過虛擬實境運動軟體或者是線上課程教學，來達到增加社會互動以及促進更好的訓練效果。讓我們參考這篇報導！連結在此喔：https://youtu.be/G9ScfKCTNak "

item.title: NSCA's Essential of Sport Science
Science and Practice of Strength Training
The most outstanding self-learning community
item.description:
Today, we accept the most outstanding self-learning community award in university of Taipei with profound gratitude. In addition, we can’t describe how honored we are to receive this award!

Most import of all, we will accept the prize as a tribute to Dr. Yu-Kai Chang (Research Chair Professor, Department of Physical Education and Sport Sciences, National Taiwan Normal University), Dr. Ching-Feng Cheng (Professor, Department of Athletic Performance, National Taiwan Normal University), Dr. Yi-Hung Liao (Professor, Department of Exercise and Health Science, National Taipei University of Nursing and Health Sciences), and Dr. Hsin-Fu Lin (Professor, Sport Facility Management and Health Promotion, National Taiwan University). Thank you all for novel and marvelous speech!

While this award is being given to me, as a matter of fact, it should go to our team of colleagues and PI, Dr. Chung-Yu Chen (Professor & Director of R&D office, Department of Exercise and Health Sciences, University of Taipei), without whom none of this would have been possible! Let’s keep moving forward with strong and active faith in exercise science, sport science, and health science!

item.title: The role
item.description:
As a practitioner in exercise science, mindset is very important! We are not only the experiment conductor but also the participant sometimes instead. Both roles all lead to the the central role of exercise science. How we balance these two role might be the issue for featured research.

As professor Liao always reminds us that: “Something very small or minor can be indicative of something much more important.”

In other words, an experiment is not just an experiment, it’s about how well you organize and prepare for the oncoming difficulty and possible defeat. Life isn’t all sunshine and rainbows, how much we can take the challenge and keep moving forward would be the key!

item.title: Academic Plan in Doctoral Degree
item.description:
To be updated.

My future academic plan in doctoral degree will be described in this post. I will explain my future career as the following aspects:

  1. Academic Performance
    2. Fitness Industry Performance

The aim of Doctoral Training might be research, but NOT only for research itself.

In my opinion, the main aim of doctoral training is to help PhD researchers to think and act more creatively and independently.

This will prepare them for a career in frontier research and education or for higher level roles in professional sectors where in-depth and rigorous analysis is required.

In order to achieve this aim, the doctoral training should focus on personal development in 4 complementary areas:

  1. Professional expertise a.Knowledge of the exercise science and health science discipline b.Analytic and synthesizing skills c.creativity thinking d.out-of-the-box thinking (critical thinking)

  2. Research management skills a.Project management and financing sponsorship consideration b.respecting professional and ethical standards

  3. Personal effectiveness a.Self-management (Self-disciplined) b.time management c.motivation d.problem solving e.career planning

  4. Impact and influence a.Collaboration communication b.networking c.leadership

Research-based training & structured training activities by the Doctoral School

On the other side, Doctoral training takes place first and foremost in the research group or laboratory via informal and formal meetings with the supervisor and fellow researchers. This research-based training is complemented by more structured training activities organized by the Doctoral School.

The structured part of the doctoral training consists of 3 components:

  1. Research skills.

  2. Thematic training in a doctoral school programs comprising advanced courses, invited lectures, journal clubs and doctoral seminars focused on a specific biomedical topic.

  3. Training and development of transferable skills that are relevant for the successful completion of the doctorate and can be valorised later in different professional sectors.
